content_en:
Among the most important theoretical distinctions in the science of hadith is the division between Mutawatir (mass-transmitted) narrations and Ahad (single or limited-chain) narrations. This distinction has significant consequences not only for hadith science but for Islamic jurisprudence and theology, because the epistemological status of a narration — whether it produces definitive knowledge (ilm qat'i) or probable knowledge (ilm dhanni) — determines how it can be used in deriving religious rulings and beliefs.

A Mutawatir hadith is one transmitted by such a large number of independent narrators at every level of the isnad that their agreement on a lie or error is rationally impossible. The number required for tawatur has been debated by scholars, with some specifying numbers (ten, twelve, twenty, forty, or more) and others leaving it undefined but requiring only that the number be sufficient to preclude collusion. What is agreed is that a true mutawatir narration produces definitive certain knowledge (ilm qat'i), and denying a mutawatir narration — if one is aware of its status — is a serious theological error, because it denies what has been established with certainty.

Examples of mutawatir narrations include: the description of raising the hands in prayer, the practice of wiping over leather socks (khuffayn), the reports about the punishment of the grave, the physical characteristics of the Day of Judgment, and many narrations regarding the fundamental practices of Islamic worship. The Quran itself is the supreme example of tawatur — every verse has been transmitted by thousands upon thousands of narrators throughout every generation, making the Quran the most mutawatir text in human history.

Ahad narrations, by contrast, are those transmitted through chains too limited to reach the threshold of tawatur. They are subdivided into: Mashhur (widely known/famous) — transmitted by three or more narrators at every level but not reaching tawatur; Aziz (rare) — transmitted by exactly two narrators at every level; and Gharib (strange/singular) — transmitted by only one narrator at some or all levels of the chain.

The epistemological status of Ahad narrations has been debated throughout Islamic intellectual history. The dominant position of the Sunni fuqaha (jurists) is that authenticated Ahad narrations produce probable knowledge (al-zann al-ghalib) sufficient to establish legal obligations and prohibitions — the vast majority of Islamic law is derived from Ahad narrations. The Mu'tazilah and some later scholars argued that Ahad narrations cannot establish theological beliefs (aqeedah), though the majority of Sunni theologians, particularly the Hanbalis, hold that authenticated Ahad narrations establish beliefs in all domains. Ibn al-Salah's Muqaddimah takes the position that the highest-grade Ahad narrations — those found in the Sahihayn — produce a special kind of certainty through the consensus of the ummah's acceptance of them.
content_id:
Di antara pembedaan teoritis terpenting dalam ilmu hadis adalah pembagian antara narasi-narasi Mutawatir (diriwayatkan secara masif) dan Ahad (rantai tunggal atau terbatas). Pembedaan ini memiliki konsekuensi penting tidak hanya bagi ilmu hadis tetapi juga bagi yurisprudensi Islam dan epistemologi keagamaan.

Hadis Mutawatir didefinisikan sebagai hadis yang diriwayatkan oleh sekelompok besar perawi dari sekelompok besar perawi lainnya di setiap tingkat rantai periwayatan, sehingga tidak mungkin secara rasional mereka semua bersepakat untuk berdusta. Hadis mutawatir memberikan ilmu yakin (keyakinan yang pasti) dan wajib diterima secara akidah.

Contoh-contoh hadis mutawatir antara lain: hadis-hadis tentang Haudh (telaga Nabi ﷺ di surga), hadis-hadis tentang syafaat Nabi ﷺ, hadis tentang mengangkat kedua tangan dalam doa, dan berbagai keterangan tentang rukun-rukun shalat.

Hadis Ahad adalah hadis yang tidak mencapai derajat mutawatir — diriwayatkan oleh satu, dua, atau sejumlah kecil perawi. Para ulama membagi hadis ahad menjadi tiga tingkatan berdasarkan jumlah jalur periwayatan: masyhur (terkenal/tiga atau lebih jalur), 'aziz (jarang/dua jalur), dan gharib (asing/satu jalur).

Hadis ahad yang shahih memberikan zhan ghalib (dugaan yang kuat) dan wajib diamalkan dalam fiqih dan akhlak. Namun para ulama berbeda pendapat tentang apakah hadis ahad dapat dijadikan dasar akidah — mayoritas ulama memperbolehkannya jika hadis tersebut shahih dan maknanya tidak bertentangan dengan Al-Quran.
